title: Dielectric response and viscosity due to dipolar interactions
summary: The dielectric response and viscosity are two fundamental properties of liquids that are usually treated separately. Here we show that in highly polar liquids the viscosity can be predicted directly from the dielectric function. We employ a stochastic field theory for thermal dipole-field dynamics coupled to hydrodynamic flow, and derive a very general Kubo relation for the response of an observable to the flow. We then use this to derive a Green-Kubo formula for the viscosity operator in terms of the correlation function for the body force, rather than the usual stress tensor formulation, and from this we derive the contribution to the viscosity due to dipolar interactions. In strongly polar liquids like water we show that viscous dissipation arising from these thermal van der Waals interactions is the dominant dissipative mechanism, leading to a direct connection between dielectric relaxation and viscosity. The theory also predicts the emergence of a second relaxation time in the dielectric response even when only a single microscopic relaxation mechanism is present. This additional timescale contributes to the intrinsic Debye relaxation and provides a natural explanation for the widespread empirical observation that many liquids require two relaxation times to fit their dielectric spectra. By establishing a predictive link between dielectric properties and viscosity, our results revisit classical ideas of liquid dynamics originating with Debye and suggest a practical route for identifying promising solvents for electrochemical energy storage.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes a predictive link between dielectric response and viscosity in polar liquids, showing that viscosity can be predicted from dielectric function and explaining why two relaxation times appear in dielectric spectra even with single microscopic mechanism.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between dielectric response and viscosity - two fundamental liquid properties typically treated separately - and revisit classical ideas of liquid dynamics from Debye's era, with practical applications for electrochemical energy storage solvents.
AI.method: Developed a stochastic field theory for thermal dipole-field dynamics coupled to hydrodynamic flow, derived a general Kubo relation for observable response to flow, and obtained a Green-Kubo formula for viscosity operator using body force correlation functions rather than stress tensor.
AI.result: In strongly polar liquids like water, viscous dissipation from thermal van der Waals interactions dominates, creating direct connection between dielectric relaxation and viscosity. Theory predicts emergence of second relaxation time in dielectric response even with single microscopic mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Establishes predictive link between dielectric properties and viscosity, explains widespread empirical observation of two relaxation times in dielectric spectra, and provides practical route for identifying promising solvents for electrochemical energy storage.

title: Self-similar summation of virial expansions
summary: Virial expansions are the series in powers of density assumed to be small. However, the equations of state require to consider finite densities for which virial expansions, as a rule, diverge. In order to extrapolate a virial expansion to the values, where this expansion diverges, one uses summation methods. The most often used method is the Padé summation, which has several deficiencies. First of all, Padé approximants are not uniquely defined, suggesting a large table of admissible variants. Second, often there appear spurious unphysical poles. On the contrary, in those cases where the existence of a pole is physically motivated, Padé approximants do not necessarily exhibit it. A new approach for the summation of virial expansions is suggested, based on self-similar approximation theory. The method is regular and uniquely defined. It allows for the determination of physically motivated poles. The accuracy of self-similar approximants is not worse than that of the best Padé approximants with fitting parameters or of Monte Carlo simulations. The self-similar summation is based solely on virial expansions, involving no fitting parameters. In some cases, self-similar summation allows for reconstructing the sought functions exactly. The approach is illustrated by summing virial expansions for hard-disk fluids, hard-sphere fluids, and systems with power-law potentials.

AI.tldr: A new self-similar approximation theory method for summing virial expansions that overcomes deficiencies of Padé summation, providing unique, regular solutions with physically motivated poles and no fitting parameters.
AI.motivation: Virial expansions diverge at finite densities where equations of state are needed. Padé summation has deficiencies: non-unique solutions, spurious unphysical poles, and failure to capture physically motivated poles. A more robust summation method is required.
AI.method: Self-similar approximation theory is proposed as a new summation approach. The method is regular and uniquely defined, allows determination of physically motivated poles, uses no fitting parameters, and works solely from virial expansion coefficients.
AI.result: Self-similar approximants achieve accuracy comparable to or better than best Padé approximants with fitting parameters or Monte Carlo simulations. In some cases, the method can reconstruct exact functions. Demonstrated successful application to hard-disk fluids, hard-sphere fluids, and systems with power-law potentials.
AI.conclusion: Self-similar summation provides a superior alternative to Padé summation for virial expansions, offering unique, physically meaningful solutions without fitting parameters while maintaining or improving accuracy across various fluid systems.

title: Unified Gauge-Geometry Symmetry for Equilibrium Statistical Mechanics
summary: We present a symmetry-based framework for equilibrium statistical mechanics that formulates a single Lie group combining conventional spacetime symmetries with a recently identified phase-space gauge-shifting invariance [Muller et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 133, 217101 (2024)]. Using Noether's theorem, we obtain a set of general Ward identities together with previously unexplored cross-relations arising from the noncommutation of different symmetry generators. The approach extends standard many-body symmetries, such as translations, rotations, Galilean boosts, dilations, and particle exchange, by incorporating an internal gauge-shift symmetry within a unified group structure. The resulting Lie algebra suggests a hierarchy of exact identities that encompass established sum rules and indicate possible cross-coupling relations between distinct response and correlation functions. We also identify a Wigner-Eckart-Ward reduction that simplifies tensor-hyperforce correlators to two scalar radial spectra in isotropic fluids, and we outline an equivariant gauge-constrained DFT formulation whose Euler-Lagrange equations are constructed to satisfy the corresponding Ward and cross-Ward constraints. This framework provides a consistent organizational basis for phenomena in liquids, mixtures, and interfaces, and may offer a symmetry-based perspective connecting structure, mechanics, and dynamics in many-body systems.

AI.tldr: A symmetry-based framework for statistical mechanics that unifies conventional spacetime symmetries with phase-space gauge-shifting invariance, yielding Ward identities and cross-relations through Lie group theory.
AI.motivation: To develop a comprehensive symmetry framework that extends standard many-body symmetries by incorporating internal gauge-shift symmetry, providing a unified organizational basis for understanding structure, mechanics, and dynamics in many-body systems.
AI.method: Formulates a single Lie group combining conventional spacetime symmetries (translations, rotations, Galilean boosts, dilations, particle exchange) with phase-space gauge-shifting invariance, applies Noether's theorem to derive Ward identities and cross-relations from noncommuting symmetry generators, and develops gauge-constrained DFT with Euler-Lagrange equations satisfying Ward constraints.
AI.result: Obtains general Ward identities and previously unexplored cross-relations, identifies a hierarchy of exact identities encompassing established sum rules, suggests cross-coupling between response and correlation functions, derives Wigner-Eckart-Ward reduction simplifying tensor-hyperforce correlators to two scalar radial spectra in isotropic fluids.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a consistent organizational basis for phenomena in liquids, mixtures, and interfaces, offering a symmetry-based perspective connecting structure, mechanics, and dynamics in many-body systems through unified Lie group theory.

title: Anomalous waiting-time distributions in postselection-free quantum many-body dynamics under continuous monitoring
comment: 18 pages, submitted to "Focus on Non-Hermitian Quantum Many-Body Physics" in Quantum Science and Technology
summary: We investigate waiting-time distributions (WTDs) of quantum jumps in continuously monitored quantum many-body systems, whose unconditional dynamics lead to the trivial infinite-temperature state. We demonstrate that the WTD of a half-chain subsystem exhibits an anomalous tail, markedly deviating from the Poissonian distribution in stark contrast to that of the whole system. By analyzing the spectral properties of the superoperator $\mathscr L_0$, which is defined by removing the jump terms associated with the half-chain subsystem from the full Liouvillian, we find that the long-time behavior with the anomalous tail of the half-chain WTD is governed by the eigenvalue $λ_0\:(<0)$ with the largest real part. We further reveal a qualitative change in the system-size dependence of $λ_0$ as a function of the measurement strength: for sufficiently weak measurement, $λ_0$ decreases proportionally to the system size, while for strong measurement, $λ_0$ scales independently of the system size, signaling the persistence of the anomalous half-chain WTD in the thermodynamic limit. The WTD is extracted solely from the spacetime record of quantum jumps $\{t_i,x_i\}$ and can be experimentally accessed without postselection. Our work establishes a spectral framework for understanding nontrivial WTDs in subsystems of monitored quantum dynamics and provides a novel diagnostics to assess many-body effects on WTDs.

AI.tldr: Half-chain quantum jump waiting-time distributions in monitored many-body systems show anomalous non-Poissonian tails governed by spectral properties of a modified Liouvillian, with measurement strength determining system-size scaling.
AI.motivation: To understand waiting-time distributions (WTDs) of quantum jumps in continuously monitored quantum many-body systems that thermalize to infinite temperature, focusing on subsystem WTDs that may exhibit nontrivial behavior despite trivial unconditional dynamics.
AI.method: Analyze spectral properties of superoperator $\mathscr L_0$ (full Liouvillian minus half-chain jump terms) to study WTDs. Extract WTDs from spacetime records of quantum jumps $\{t_i,x_i\}$ without postselection. Investigate system-size dependence of leading eigenvalue $\lambda_0$ as function of measurement strength.
AI.result: Half-chain WTDs show anomalous non-Poissonian tails governed by eigenvalue $\lambda_0$ of $\mathscr L_0$. Measurement strength determines scaling: weak measurement yields $\lambda_0 \propto$ system size, strong measurement yields system-size independent $\lambda_0$, indicating persistent anomalous WTDs in thermodynamic limit.
AI.conclusion: Establishes spectral framework for understanding nontrivial WTDs in subsystems of monitored quantum dynamics. Provides novel diagnostics for assessing many-body effects on WTDs, with WTDs accessible experimentally without postselection.

title: On measurement, superdeterminism, free will, and contextuality
comment: 14 pages (12 plus title page and table of contents)
summary: Superdeterminism has received recent attention as a possible path toward a locally causal explanation of the entanglement correlations that appear in experimental tests of Bell's theorem. While the term `superdeterminism' was coined by Bell to refer to restrictions on the free will of experimenters, it was not rigorously defined until recently. It has now been defined as a property of any physical theory that produces systematic violations of statistical independence. Here we focus on formalizing the requirements that being nonsuperdeterministic places on a physical theory, and setting a standard that must be met before we can conclude that a given theory is not superdeterministic. We begin by carefully examining how a physical theory determines what outcomes we observe when performing measurements in terms of ontic states and response functions, and how this differs between superdeterministic and nonsuperdeterministic theories, in terms of the behavior of the types of vetted random sampling procedures that we use in experiments. The core result is that individual samples and measurement outcomes must be representative of the observed distributions, which is explained in detail. This also has a bearing on how measurement settings are chosen by agents, whether freely or randomly, and we argue that this standard ultimately defines what freedom/independence actually mean. We then discuss contextuality, and show that in most cases, superdeterminism is contextual. Finally, we discuss how different physical theories, with different notions of ontic states and response functions, can give rise to the same empirical data, and how the same operational contextuality may appear in different forms.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes requirements for nonsuperdeterministic theories, establishing standards to distinguish them from superdeterministic ones by analyzing how theories determine measurement outcomes through ontic states and response functions.
AI.motivation: To provide rigorous formalization of what constitutes nonsuperdeterminism in physical theories, addressing the need for clear standards to determine when a theory is not superdeterministic, particularly in the context of Bell's theorem violations and entanglement correlations.
AI.method: Careful examination of how physical theories determine measurement outcomes through ontic states and response functions, analyzing differences between superdeterministic and nonsuperdeterministic theories in terms of vetted random sampling procedures used in experiments.
AI.result: Establishes that individual samples and measurement outcomes must be representative of observed distributions, defines what freedom/independence actually mean in terms of measurement setting choices, and demonstrates that superdeterminism is typically contextual.
AI.conclusion: Provides formal standards for identifying nonsuperdeterministic theories, clarifies the relationship between superdeterminism and contextuality, and shows how different physical theories with different ontic states can produce the same empirical data while exhibiting the same operational contextuality.

title: Lindblad-Deformed Spectral Geometry: Heat-Kernel Asymptotics and Effective Spectral Dimension
summary: We introduce a Lindblad-deformed spectral geometric framework in which bounded dissipative data deform a standard spectral triple through the Dirac operator D_gamma = D - igammaSigma, where Sigma = (1/2) sum_k L_k^dagger L_k is constructed from Lindblad jump operators {L_k}. The associated positive operator Q_gamma = D_gamma^* D_gamma = D^2 + gamma^2 Sigma^2 - i*gamma [D, Sigma] is identified as the correct spectral-geometric observable. For smooth endomorphism-valued Lindblad data, Q_gamma is of Laplace type and admits a standard heat-kernel asymptotic expansion with dissipation-modified even Seeley-DeWitt coefficients. For the scalar deformation L = sqrt(gamma) f with f in C^infty(M) real-valued, we prove that the first-order Duhamel correction to the heat trace K_gamma(sigma) = Tr(exp(-sigma Q_gamma)) vanishes identically, so that the first nontrivial dissipative effect appears at order gamma^4. We identify the exact Duhamel-level decomposition of the O(gamma^4) correction into a direct W_2 insertion and a quadratic W_1 x W_1 term. In the round S^2 model we determine the explicit deformed operator and extract the leading local asymptotic contribution of the W_2 sector. We define the effective scale-dependent spectral dimension d_{s,eff}(sigma,gamma) = -2 d/d(log sigma) log K_gamma(sigma) and identify its leading perturbative deformation.

AI.tldr: A spectral geometric framework incorporating Lindblad dissipation via deformed Dirac operator D_γ = D - iγΣ, with Σ from Lindblad jump operators, enabling study of dissipative effects on spectral geometry and heat kernel asymptotics.
AI.motivation: To develop a mathematical framework that combines spectral geometry (noncommutative geometry) with open quantum system dynamics described by Lindblad master equations, allowing systematic study of dissipative effects on geometric structures.
AI.method: Introduce Lindblad-deformed spectral triple with Dirac operator D_γ = D - iγΣ, where Σ = (1/2)∑_k L_k^†L_k from Lindblad jump operators {L_k}. Study the positive operator Q_γ = D_γ^*D_γ = D² + γ²Σ² - iγ[D,Σ] as spectral-geometric observable. Analyze heat kernel asymptotics, prove vanishing of first-order Duhamel correction for scalar deformations, and extract O(γ⁴) corrections via Duhamel decomposition.
AI.result: For smooth endomorphism-valued Lindblad data, Q_γ is Laplace-type with dissipation-modified Seeley-DeWitt coefficients. For scalar deformation L = √γ f, first-order Duhamel correction vanishes identically, with first nontrivial dissipative effect at order γ⁴. Exact decomposition of O(γ⁴) correction into direct W₂ insertion and quadratic W₁×W₁ term. For round S² model, explicit deformed operator determined and leading local asymptotic contribution of W₂ sector extracted. Effective scale-dependent spectral dimension d_{s,eff}(σ,γ) defined and leading perturbative deformation identified.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully integrates Lindblad dissipation into spectral geometry, providing systematic tools to study dissipative effects on geometric observables. The vanishing of first-order corrections and emergence of effects at γ⁴ reveals nontrivial structure, with applications to understanding geometry in open quantum systems and potential connections to dissipative quantum gravity models.

title: Emergent Macroscopic Nonreciprocity from Identical Active Particles via Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking
summary: Nonreciprocity is known to generate a wide range of exotic phenomena in multi-species many-body systems, where different species influence one another through couplings that violate Newton's third law. In contrast, in the absence of explicitly imposed macroscopic nonreciprocal processes, single-species nonreciprocity -- another distinct form of nonreciprocity -- typically plays only a limited role in shaping macroscopic physics. Here, using a single-species Vicsek model with a vision cone and extrinsic noise, we show that spontaneous symmetry breaking (SSB) can dramatically enhance the macroscopic consequences of microscopic single-species nonreciprocity. In the ordered phase, this enhancement gives rise to an emergent macroscopic nonreciprocity that induces the system of identical active particles to admit an effective description with a "two-species" non-Hermitian structure. The resulting SSB-enhanced nonreciprocity substantially promotes traveling-band formation and, more strikingly, drives a novel real-space condensation of identical active particles, characterized by a "traveling line" with vanishing longitudinal width. Our findings uncover a fundamental mechanism by which microscopic single-species nonreciprocity can exert strong macroscopic influences in complex systems.

AI.tldr: Single-species nonreciprocity in active matter systems becomes macroscopically significant when enhanced by spontaneous symmetry breaking, leading to emergent non-Hermitian physics, traveling bands, and novel condensation phenomena.
AI.motivation: To understand how microscopic single-species nonreciprocity, which typically has limited macroscopic effects, can become significant through spontaneous symmetry breaking in active matter systems, and to explore the resulting emergent phenomena.
AI.method: Modified single-species Vicsek model with vision cone and extrinsic noise to study how spontaneous symmetry breaking enhances microscopic nonreciprocity effects in active particle systems.
AI.result: Spontaneous symmetry breaking dramatically enhances microscopic single-species nonreciprocity, creating emergent macroscopic nonreciprocity with effective two-species non-Hermitian structure, promoting traveling-band formation and driving novel real-space condensation into "traveling lines" with vanishing longitudinal width.
AI.conclusion: Spontaneous symmetry breaking serves as a fundamental mechanism that amplifies microscopic single-species nonreciprocity to produce strong macroscopic effects, including emergent non-Hermitian physics and novel condensation phenomena in active matter systems.

title: Understanding Quantum Theory: An Operational Reconstructive Approach
comment: Forthcoming in J. Faye and L. Johansson, "How to Understand Quantum Mechanics: 100 years of Ongoing Interpretations" (Springer, 2026). Abstract abbreviated
summary:
One hundred years after the creation of quantum theory, there is no consensus on the kind of reality that is described by the theory. Here, I attribute the lack of progress to the prevailing interpretative methodology, which invariably takes the quantum formalism as the starting point for philosophical reflection and analysis. I argue that this methodology is particularly inappropriate, for it invariably marginalizes much of the theory's content, both that implicit in modelling heuristics and experimental practices, and that encapsulated in the mathematical structures of its formalism. In addition, the prevailing methodology offers little protection against undue influence by metaphysically-laden language which invariably accompanies the formalism.
  Here, I summarize an alternative methodology whose goal is to ensure that an interpretational project take into account all forms of theoretic content. The methodology harnesses the recent results of the quantum reconstruction program. These results distil the mathematical content of the quantum formalism into physical principles and assumptions, which are more readily philosophically digestible than the formalism itself, and bracket much of its metaphysically-laden language.
  As a case study of reconstruction-based interpretation, I describe the reconstruction of the identical particle formalism, and its step-by-step interpretation, highlighting the key questions that drive the interpretation forwards and the techniques and stances that are employed in each step. The interpretation yields a novel metaphysical profile for systems of identical particles as potential parts of a whole, which can be traced step-by-step to elementary experimental data and the reconstruction's physical postulates and assumptions. I also describe some of the pitfalls that one faces in any attempt to directly interpret the identical particle formalism.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques current quantum interpretation methodology for being formalism-centric and proposes an alternative reconstruction-based approach that uses distilled physical principles to guide interpretation, demonstrated through identical particle systems.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of consensus on quantum reality after a century, the author identifies the problem as the prevailing interpretative methodology that starts from the quantum formalism, which marginalizes important theoretical content and is vulnerable to metaphysically-laden language.
AI.method: Proposes an alternative methodology based on quantum reconstruction program results, which distills mathematical formalism into physical principles and assumptions. Uses identical particle formalism reconstruction as a case study, with step-by-step interpretation linking to experimental data and physical postulates.
AI.result: The reconstruction-based interpretation yields a novel metaphysical profile for identical particle systems as "potential parts of a whole," with clear traceability to experimental data and physical postulates. Demonstrates pitfalls of direct formalism interpretation.
AI.conclusion: Quantum reconstruction provides a superior methodology for interpretation by making physical principles philosophically digestible, avoiding formalism-centric pitfalls, and ensuring comprehensive consideration of theoretical content, as shown in the identical particle case study.

title: QR-SPPS: Quantum-Native Retail Supply Chain Risk Simulation via VQE, ADAPT-VQE Counterfactual Policy Ranking, and DOS-QPE Boltzmann Tail Risk Quantification
comment: 7 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables, 2 algorithms. Implemented in Qiskit with Aer statevector_simulator and OpenFermion. Cross-list to cs.ET and quant-ph requested
summary: Classical supply chain risk models treat node failures as statistically independent events, systematically underestimating cascade probabilities when supplier dependencies are strongly correlated. At n=40 nodes, the full correlated failure distribution requires O(2^n) classical samples, a regime where exact simulation demands 17.6 TB of memory and over 369,000 hours of computation on a standard workstation. We present QR-SPPS (Quantum-Native Retail Shock Propagation and Policy Stress Simulator), a three-algorithm quantum pipeline implemented using the Qiskit framework with the Aer statevector_simulator backend. First, a 40-node, 4-tier retail supply network is encoded as a 40-qubit Ising Hamiltonian using OpenFermion QubitOperator, where ZZ coupling terms encode correlated cascade probabilities structurally absent from classical Monte Carlo. Second, a hardware-efficient VQE circuit finds the ground-state stress distribution with zero error, detecting entangled cascade failures in 14/40 nodes with max|ΔP|=0.637 versus classical Monte Carlo. Third, we introduce the first application of ADAPT-VQE gradient screening to counterfactual macroeconomic policy evaluation: six crisis interventions are ranked in O(1) Qiskit operator evaluations per policy, a 287x speedup over sequential VQE re-optimisation. Fourth, Density-of-States QPE (DOS-QPE) reconstructs the full eigenspectrum via 32-step Trotter evolution and introduces a novel mapping of the Boltzmann catastrophe probability P_cat(T) to VIX-equivalent market volatility temperature, enabling direct integration into regulatory Value-at-Risk frameworks. Qiskit Aer scaling benchmarks confirm exponential classical intractability at 40 qubits.

AI.tldr: QR-SPPS is a quantum pipeline using 40-qubit Ising models to simulate correlated supply chain failures, achieving exponential speedup over classical methods and enabling policy evaluation with 287x speedup.
AI.motivation: Classical supply chain risk models fail to capture correlated cascade failures due to statistical independence assumptions, requiring O(2^n) classical samples that become computationally intractable at 40 nodes (17.6 TB memory, 369k hours computation).
AI.method: Three-algorithm quantum pipeline: 1) Encode 40-node retail network as 40-qubit Ising Hamiltonian using OpenFermion QubitOperator; 2) Use hardware-efficient VQE to find ground-state stress distribution; 3) Apply ADAPT-VQE gradient screening for policy evaluation; 4) Use DOS-QPE to reconstruct full eigenspectrum via 32-step Trotter evolution.
AI.result: VQE detects entangled cascade failures in 14/40 nodes with max|ΔP|=0.637 vs classical Monte Carlo; ADAPT-VQE achieves 287x speedup for policy evaluation; DOS-QPE maps Boltzmann catastrophe probability to VIX-equivalent market volatility temperature; Qiskit benchmarks confirm exponential classical intractability at 40 qubits.
AI.conclusion: QR-SPPS demonstrates quantum advantage for correlated supply chain risk modeling, enabling accurate cascade failure prediction and efficient policy evaluation that integrates directly into regulatory Value-at-Risk frameworks.

title: Tunable information insulation induced by constraint mismatch
summary: We study a composite model of two $1D$ $PXP$ chains with dual constraints, forming a junction that acts as an infinite kinematic barrier to quantum information exchange. Moreover, the hard wall at the junction which acts as a perfect reflector, preventing any quantum information leakage between the two sides of the composite chain, can be made permeable by relaxing the constraint at the junction sites. Multiple frozen junctions shatter the Hilbert space into disjoint Krylov fragments, the number of which increases exponentially with the engineered defects. Furthermore, the energy level statistics in each symmetry-resolved sector are strictly Poissonian, demonstrating that the tensor sum of the disjoint Hamiltonians results in a pure superposition of the chaotic spectra of the sub- $PXP$ chains. We also find that a chirally protected zero-energy mode can exist which has local peaks at the physical edges and within the bulk near the junction sites. This state is protected from hybridization with bulk states induced by any chirality preserving disorder. Due to the tensor product structure of the eigenfunctions, the non-zero energy scar states also multiply in number. Finally, we introduce novel Fock states with spatially tunable thermal and athermal regions. This architecture can be readily realized in programmable Rydberg atom platforms using optical tweezers, addressing beams and facilitation techniques.

AI.tldr: A composite model of two 1D PXP chains with dual constraints creates an infinite kinematic barrier at the junction, preventing quantum information exchange. Relaxing constraints makes the barrier permeable, while multiple frozen junctions exponentially fragment the Hilbert space into Krylov subspaces with Poissonian statistics. The system exhibits chirally protected zero-energy modes, proliferated scar states, and novel Fock states with tunable thermal/athermal regions, realizable in Rydberg atom platforms.
AI.motivation: To study quantum information dynamics in constrained systems, specifically how engineered defects and junction constraints affect information exchange, Hilbert space fragmentation, and the emergence of protected modes in composite PXP chains.
AI.method: Construct a composite model of two 1D PXP chains with dual constraints at the junction. Analyze the system by: 1) studying the junction as an infinite kinematic barrier, 2) relaxing constraints to make it permeable, 3) introducing multiple frozen junctions to fragment Hilbert space into Krylov subspaces, 4) examining energy level statistics in symmetry-resolved sectors, 5) identifying chirally protected zero-energy modes, and 6) analyzing tensor product structure of eigenfunctions and scar states.
AI.result: 1) The junction acts as a perfect reflector preventing quantum information leakage between chains. 2) Relaxing constraints makes the barrier permeable. 3) Multiple frozen junctions exponentially increase Krylov fragments with Poissonian statistics. 4) Chirally protected zero-energy modes exist at edges and near junctions, immune to chirality-preserving disorder. 5) Non-zero energy scar states multiply due to tensor product structure. 6) Novel Fock states with spatially tunable thermal and athermal regions can be engineered.
AI.conclusion: The composite PXP chain architecture provides a versatile platform for controlling quantum information exchange, Hilbert space fragmentation, and protected modes through engineered constraints. The system demonstrates how tensor sum structures yield pure superpositions of chaotic spectra and how chirally protected modes can be localized. This architecture is experimentally realizable in programmable Rydberg atom platforms using optical tweezers and facilitation techniques.

title: Zeno-Constrained Formation of Relativistic Mass Shells
comment: This Manuscript has been accepted by "Open System & Information Dynamics"
summary: We study an extension of the quantum linear Boltzmann equation describing irreversible momentum-space dynamics of an open quantum system under strong continuous monitoring. The monitored observable is taken to be a quadratic form in an extended, purely Euclidean four-dimensional momentum space, without assuming any fixed signature at the microscopic level. In the resulting quantum Zeno regime, rapid suppression of off-constraint excursions allows for an adiabatic elimination of fast degrees of freedom. Using a Schur-complement construction, the induced second-order corrections give rise to an effective flow of the monitored quadratic form under temporal coarse graining. Under mild isotropy assumptions on the underlying momentum-mixing dynamics and an appropriate calibration condition, this flow approaches an infrared fixed point characterized by a quadratic form of Lorentzian signature. The corresponding null set defines a mass-shell-like constraint surface that governs the long-time Zeno-projected dynamics and whose isometry group matches the kinematic structure of Lorentz transformations at the effective level. Familiar relativistic features, including Maxwell-Juettner-type stationary distributions, arise at the level of the effective infrared description as consequences of this fixed point within the extended quantum Boltzmann framework.

AI.tldr: Quantum linear Boltzmann equation extended to include strong continuous monitoring of quadratic momentum observables leads to emergent Lorentzian structure through quantum Zeno effects and infrared fixed points.
AI.motivation: To understand how relativistic kinematic structures can emerge from non-relativistic quantum dynamics under continuous monitoring, particularly investigating whether Lorentz symmetry can arise as an effective description from more fundamental quantum processes without assuming it a priori.
AI.method: Extends quantum linear Boltzmann equation with strong continuous monitoring of quadratic forms in 4D Euclidean momentum space. Uses quantum Zeno regime to suppress off-constraint excursions, adiabatic elimination of fast degrees of freedom, Schur-complement construction for second-order corrections, and temporal coarse-graining to derive effective flow equations.
AI.result: Under isotropy assumptions and calibration conditions, the flow approaches an infrared fixed point characterized by quadratic form with Lorentzian signature. The null set defines a mass-shell constraint surface whose isometry group matches Lorentz transformations. Relativistic features like Maxwell-Juettner distributions emerge at the effective infrared description.
AI.conclusion: Lorentzian kinematic structure emerges naturally from quantum dynamics under continuous monitoring as an infrared fixed point, suggesting that relativistic physics can arise as an effective description from more fundamental quantum processes without requiring Lorentz symmetry at the microscopic level.

title: Negative Differential Heat Conductivity in a Harmonic Chain Coupled to a Particle Reservoir
summary: When coupling thermal baths at different temperatures, negative differential thermal conductivity is typically attributed to nonlinear interactions in the connecting medium. In this work, we demonstrate that such an effect can arise purely from the nature of the thermal baths and their coupling with the medium. Specifically, we construct a bath composed of overdamped thermal particles, which is coupled to one end of a harmonic chain, while the other end is connected to a standard Langevin heat bath. By analyzing the steady-state heat current, we observe significant negative differential thermal conductivity. In particular, as the temperature difference between the two baths diverges, the steady-state heat current through the chain vanishes. The effect is thermokinetic: we compute the effective dissipative coefficient and we find that it scales inversely with the square of the temperature of the particle bath in the high-temperature limit, resulting in an asymptotic decoupling between the bath and the chain. Our results highlight that nonequilibrium transport properties can be strongly influenced by the structure of the environment and its coupling to the system, even in otherwise linear systems.

AI.tldr: Negative differential thermal conductivity can arise from bath structure and coupling alone, without nonlinear interactions, as demonstrated with an overdamped particle bath coupled to a harmonic chain.
AI.motivation: To show that negative differential thermal conductivity (NDTC) can emerge purely from the nature of thermal baths and their coupling to the connecting medium, challenging the typical attribution of NDTC to nonlinear interactions in the medium itself.
AI.method: Construct a system with two different thermal baths: one composed of overdamped thermal particles coupled to one end of a harmonic chain, and a standard Langevin heat bath coupled to the other end. Analyze steady-state heat current through the harmonic chain.
AI.result: Significant negative differential thermal conductivity is observed. As temperature difference between baths diverges, steady-state heat current vanishes. The effective dissipative coefficient scales inversely with square of particle bath temperature in high-temperature limit, leading to asymptotic decoupling between bath and chain.
AI.conclusion: Nonequilibrium transport properties can be strongly influenced by bath structure and coupling to the system, even in linear systems. NDTC can arise from bath characteristics alone, not requiring nonlinear interactions in the connecting medium.

title: Conclusive Identification Via Noisy Classical Channel: Superactivation and Quantum Advantage
comment: 30.25 pages (single column) + 3.5 pages (single column), 11 figures; Comments are welcome
summary: We introduce conclusive identification task for classical channels: a receiver identifies transmitted inputs without error when possible, and responds inconclusively when outputs are ambiguous. For a symmetric not-fully-corrupted channel $N : X \to X$, the single-shot conclusive identification index $\mathrm{ci}_\circ(N)$ counts the maximum number of conclusively identifiable inputs. We show $\mathrm{ci}_\circ(N)$ exhibits a striking superactivation phenomenon: a channel with $\mathrm{ci}_\circ(N) = 0$ achieves $\mathrm{ci}_\circ(N \otimes \mathrm{id}^c_β) = |X|$ when assisted by a perfect classical channel of dimension $β< |X|$. The minimum classical assistance required equals the chromatic number $χ(\mathtt{S}_N)$ of the channel's support graph $\mathtt{S}_N$. We provide channel families where the superactivation gap $\mathrm{ci}_\circ(N \otimes \mathrm{id}^c_β) - \mathrm{ci}_\circ(\mathrm{id}^c_β)$ can be made arbitrarily large. A noiseless quantum channel of dimension equal to the orthogonal rank $ξ(\mathtt{S}_N)$ suffices, yielding a strict quantum advantage whenever $ξ(\mathtt{S}_N) < χ(\mathtt{S}_N)$. This advantage is demonstrated through three explicit constructions motivated by combinatorial and algebraic state-independent, and state-dependent proofs of Kochen-Specker contextuality. Via the co-normal product of graphs, we analyze the scaling of the quantum advantage ratio $χ_f(\mathtt{S}_N)/ξ(\mathtt{S}_N)$, and present a channel for which quantum assistance is exponentially more efficient than classical. Our results establish $\mathtt{S}_N$, rather than the confusability graph $\mathtt{G}_N$, as the natural combinatorial object for conclusive identification, revealing that channels deemed useless under Shannon's zero-error framework can exhibit rich superactivation and quantum advantage, with deep connections to quantum contextuality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces conclusive identification for classical channels, where receivers can respond inconclusively when outputs are ambiguous. It shows striking superactivation phenomena where useless channels become fully identifiable with classical assistance, connects the minimum assistance to chromatic numbers, demonstrates quantum advantages via Kochen-Specker contextuality, and establishes support graphs as the natural combinatorial framework.
AI.motivation: To study conclusive identification tasks for classical channels where receivers can give inconclusive responses when outputs are ambiguous, moving beyond Shannon's zero-error framework. The motivation is to understand how channels deemed useless in traditional zero-error communication can exhibit rich behaviors like superactivation and quantum advantage when allowing inconclusive responses.
AI.method: Introduces conclusive identification index ciₒ(N) for symmetric not-fully-corrupted channels. Analyzes superactivation phenomena where ciₒ(N) = 0 channels achieve full identification with classical assistance. Connects minimum classical assistance to chromatic number χ(S_N) of the channel's support graph. Demonstrates quantum advantage using noiseless quantum channels of dimension equal to orthogonal rank ξ(S_N). Uses combinatorial and algebraic constructions motivated by Kochen-Specker contextuality proofs. Analyzes scaling via co-normal product of graphs.
AI.result: Shows striking superactivation: channels with ciₒ(N) = 0 achieve ciₒ(N ⊗ id^c_β) = |X| with classical assistance of dimension β < |X|. Minimum classical assistance equals chromatic number χ(S_N). Demonstrates quantum advantage when ξ(S_N) < χ(S_N) using quantum channels of dimension ξ(S_N). Presents explicit constructions from Kochen-Specker contextuality. Shows quantum assistance can be exponentially more efficient than classical. Establishes support graph S_N (rather than confusability graph G_N) as the natural combinatorial object for conclusive identification.
AI.conclusion: Channels considered useless under Shannon's zero-error framework exhibit rich superactivation and quantum advantage in conclusive identification. The support graph S_N is the fundamental combinatorial structure, revealing deep connections to quantum contextuality. Quantum resources provide strict advantages over classical ones, with exponential efficiency gaps possible. This establishes conclusive identification as a framework connecting information theory, combinatorics, and quantum foundations.

title: Statistical Physics of Coding for the Integers
summary: We study a paradigm of coding for compression of the natural numbers via the zeta distribution and develop a statistical-mechanical interpretation, both in terms of Hagedorn systems and a Bose gas with energy levels given by logarithms of prime numbers. We also propose a simple coding scheme for the zeta distribution that nearly achieves the ideal code length. For block coding of vectors of natural numbers, we derive the micro-canonical entropy function and demonstrate its asymptotic linearity implying that its behavior is analogous to that of a Hagedorn system. We also derive the large deviations rate function, and provide a formula for the best coding parameter in the large deviations sense. We show that due the Hagedorn-type phase transition there is only partial equivalence of ensembles, due to the degeneration of the domain of the partition function.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a statistical-mechanical interpretation of coding for natural number compression using the zeta distribution, connecting it to Hagedorn systems and Bose gases with prime number energy levels, while analyzing phase transitions and ensemble equivalence issues.
AI.motivation: To establish connections between information theory (coding for natural number compression) and statistical mechanics, specifically through the zeta distribution, and to explore the implications of Hagedorn-type phase transitions in coding theory.
AI.method: Develops a statistical-mechanical interpretation using zeta distribution, analyzes coding schemes for natural numbers, derives micro-canonical entropy functions, examines large deviations rate functions, and studies Hagedorn systems and Bose gases with prime logarithm energy levels.
AI.result: Proposes a simple coding scheme that nearly achieves ideal code length for zeta distribution, demonstrates asymptotic linearity of micro-canonical entropy (analogous to Hagedorn systems), derives large deviations rate functions, identifies partial ensemble equivalence due to Hagedorn phase transitions, and provides optimal coding parameters.
AI.conclusion: The zeta distribution provides a natural bridge between coding theory and statistical mechanics, revealing Hagedorn-type phase transitions that lead to partial ensemble equivalence and degeneration of partition function domains, with implications for optimal coding strategies.

title: The time of arrival problem in the Page-Wootters formalism
summary: The time-of-arrival problem asks for the probability distribution for when a quantum particle reaches a specified location. It has been the subject of decades of debate, exemplifying the lack of a self-adjoint time observable in quantum theory. In the Page-Wootters framework, time is a relational quantity, emerging from correlations between a system and a clock induced by a global Hamiltonian constraint. We construct a time-of-arrival distribution by inverting the Page-Wootters approach, asking what time a clock reads given that the particle arrives at some fixed position. The result coincides with a common approach to the time-of-arrival problem, suggesting a potential relational interpretation of the latter. In addition to providing a relational description of the time-of-arrival problem, this gives an application of the Page-Wootters formalism to a concrete physical problem, and reveals some complications with its canonical interpretation as a theory of conditional probabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the quantum time-of-arrival problem by inverting the Page-Wootters relational time framework to derive arrival time distributions from clock-system correlations.
AI.motivation: The time-of-arrival problem has been debated for decades due to the lack of a self-adjoint time observable in quantum theory, highlighting fundamental issues in quantum temporal measurements.
AI.method: The authors invert the Page-Wootters framework: instead of asking when a particle arrives given clock time, they ask what time a clock reads given that the particle arrives at a fixed position, constructing time-of-arrival distributions from relational correlations.
AI.result: The derived time-of-arrival distribution coincides with common approaches to the problem, suggesting a potential relational interpretation of existing time-of-arrival formulations.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a relational description of the time-of-arrival problem, applies the Page-Wootters formalism to a concrete physical problem, and reveals complications with its canonical interpretation as a theory of conditional probabilities.

title: The Klein bottle ratio of two-dimensional ferromagnetic Potts models
summary: The weakly first-order nature of the two-dimensional 5-state ferromagnetic Potts model poses challenges for numerical study. Using density-matrix and tensor-network renormalization group methods, we investigate these transitions of the Potts-$q$ model via the Klein bottle ratio $g$ on original and dual lattices. Finite-size scaling of $g$ as a function of transverse system size $L_y$ accurately locates the critical points for $q = 4, 5, 6$. We further examine the transfer-matrix spectra and entanglement entropy, extracting central charges through toroidal and Klein bottle boundary conditions. For $q = 5$, the extracted central charge ($c \approx 1.14811$) is close to the real part of the theoretical value $c_{5\text{-Potts}} = 1.1375 \pm 0.0211 i$ predicted by complex conformal field theories. The observed drift in the scaling exponent $b$ effectively distinguishes the continuous transition from the weakly first-order regime. Furthermore, the extrapolated divergence of $g$ confirms the first-order nature of the $q=5$ Potts model.

AI.tldr: The paper uses tensor-network methods to study weakly first-order transitions in 2D Potts models, accurately locating critical points for q=4,5,6 and extracting central charges that match theoretical predictions.
AI.motivation: The weakly first-order nature of 2D 5-state ferromagnetic Potts models presents challenges for numerical investigation, requiring advanced methods to accurately characterize these transitions.
AI.method: Employ density-matrix and tensor-network renormalization group methods with Klein bottle ratio g on original and dual lattices. Use finite-size scaling of g as function of transverse system size Ly to locate critical points. Analyze transfer-matrix spectra and entanglement entropy with toroidal and Klein bottle boundary conditions to extract central charges.
AI.result: Accurately located critical points for q=4,5,6. For q=5, extracted central charge c≈1.14811 closely matches theoretical value c_{5-Potts}=1.1375±0.0211i. Observed drift in scaling exponent b distinguishes continuous from weakly first-order transitions. Extrapolated divergence of g confirms first-order nature of q=5 Potts model.
AI.conclusion: Tensor-network methods successfully characterize weakly first-order transitions in Potts models, with extracted central charges matching complex CFT predictions and scaling behaviors distinguishing transition types.

title: When level repulsion fails: non-normality and chaos in open quantum systems
comment: 21 pages, 3 figures. The supplementary material was added as an additional file
summary: For Hamiltonian systems, level statistics provide a faithful diagnostic of quantum chaos. By analogy, the statistics of the Lindbladian spectrum are often used in open quantum systems, and the Grobe-Haake-Sommers conjecture proposes that systems with chaotic classical counterparts should exhibit level repulsion in the Lindbladian spectrum. Here we point out an important flaw in this analogy: Hamiltonian and Lindbladian spectra behave differently and have distinct physical interpretations, and one should therefore not expect the latter to provide a reliable diagnostic. For Lindbladians, the late-time dynamics are not determined by the bulk of the eigenvalues but only by those eigenvalues -- and their corresponding eigenvectors -- with small real parts. Combined with the strong non-normality typical of Lindbladians, this allows situations in which the level statistics can be tuned almost arbitrarily without affecting the dynamics on either short or long time scales. We explicitly demonstrate this phenomenon and provide examples in which Ginibre level repulsion arises while the system dynamics at no time show signatures of chaos. We further relate this mechanism to the emergence of a non-Hermitian skin effect in Liouville space, linking boundary-induced eigenvector localization to the observed spectral instability. Our results show that level statistics cannot universally serve as a reliable diagnostic of quantum chaos in open quantum systems and highlight the need for alternative diagnostics that remain robust in strongly non-normal regimes.

AI.tldr: Lindbladian level statistics are unreliable for diagnosing quantum chaos in open systems due to spectral instability from non-normality, unlike Hamiltonian systems where level statistics faithfully indicate chaos.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the Grobe-Haake-Sommers conjecture that Lindbladian level statistics should diagnose quantum chaos like Hamiltonian systems, highlighting fundamental differences between Hamiltonian and Lindbladian spectra and their physical interpretations.
AI.method: Analyzes differences between Hamiltonian and Lindbladian spectral properties, demonstrates how non-normality in Lindbladians allows arbitrary tuning of level statistics without affecting dynamics, provides explicit examples showing Ginibre level repulsion without chaotic dynamics, and relates spectral instability to non-Hermitian skin effects in Liouville space.
AI.result: Shows that Lindbladian level statistics can be manipulated independently of system dynamics, with examples exhibiting Ginibre level repulsion while showing no signatures of chaos at any time scale, linking this to eigenvector localization from non-Hermitian skin effects.
AI.conclusion: Level statistics cannot universally serve as a reliable diagnostic of quantum chaos in open quantum systems due to spectral instability from non-normality, necessitating alternative diagnostics robust in strongly non-normal regimes.

title: Absence of $O (2)$ symmetry in the Vicsek model
summary: The phase transition in the Vicsek model is widely believed to be associated with spontaneous symmetry breaking of the two-dimensional rotational symmetry $O (2)$. In this paper, we revisit the original Vicsek model introduced in [Phys. Rev. Lett. 75, 1226] and demonstrate that the model lacks $O (2)$ symmetry. As a consequence, we numerically demonstrate that the phase transition reported in the original paper vanishes when the global phase is adaptively chosen.

AI.tldr: The paper challenges the conventional understanding of the Vicsek model's phase transition, showing it lacks O(2) symmetry and that the reported transition disappears when properly accounting for global phase.
AI.motivation: To re-examine the fundamental symmetry properties of the original Vicsek model and challenge the widely accepted belief that its phase transition is associated with spontaneous symmetry breaking of O(2) rotational symmetry.
AI.method: The authors revisit the original Vicsek model formulation and demonstrate mathematically that it lacks O(2) symmetry. They then perform numerical simulations where the global phase is adaptively chosen to properly account for the system's symmetry properties.
AI.result: The paper shows that when the global phase is adaptively chosen in simulations, the phase transition previously reported in the original Vicsek model vanishes, indicating that the transition was an artifact of improper symmetry treatment.
AI.conclusion: The conventional understanding of the Vicsek model's phase transition as spontaneous symmetry breaking of O(2) symmetry is incorrect, as the model fundamentally lacks this symmetry, and proper treatment of the global phase eliminates the reported transition.

title: Scattering phase shift in quantum mechanics on quantum computers: non-Hermitian systems and imaginary-time simulations
summary: To overcome the fast oscillatory behavior of correlation functions for extracting scattering phase shift in real-time quantum simulations encountered in Ref.\cite{Guo:2026qkx}, we propose and test two solutions in the present work. One is to simulate Hermitian systems in imaginary time, the other is to simulate non-Hermitian systems in real time. We demonstrate that both approaches lead to the problem of non-unitary quantum evolution which can be solved by combining two quantum algorithms: block encoding and Hadamard test. The combined quantum algorithm does not require mid-circuit measurements or adjustment of the input parameters of the Hamiltonian, and can be easily implemented on quantum computers. Both the size and length of quantum circuits grow linearly with evolution time. Numerical tests on quantum simulators show that both approaches agree with exact solutions for a sufficiently long time before the signal is lost in statistical fluctuations. The results bode well for using non-Hermitian and imaginary-time simulations to circumvent oscillations inherent in real-time simulation of other quantum systems.

AI.tldr: Two quantum computing approaches (imaginary-time Hermitian and real-time non-Hermitian simulations) overcome oscillatory correlation function issues for scattering phase shift extraction, using block encoding and Hadamard test without mid-circuit measurements.
AI.motivation: To overcome the fast oscillatory behavior of correlation functions encountered in real-time quantum simulations when extracting scattering phase shifts, which complicates accurate phase shift determination.
AI.method: Two approaches: 1) Simulating Hermitian systems in imaginary time, 2) Simulating non-Hermitian systems in real time. Both use a combined quantum algorithm of block encoding and Hadamard test to handle non-unitary evolution without mid-circuit measurements or Hamiltonian parameter adjustments.
AI.result: Numerical tests on quantum simulators show both approaches agree with exact solutions for sufficiently long times before statistical fluctuations dominate. Circuit size and length grow linearly with evolution time.
AI.conclusion: Non-Hermitian and imaginary-time simulations effectively circumvent oscillations inherent in real-time simulations of quantum systems, providing promising alternatives for scattering phase shift extraction.

title: Principal component analysis of wavefunction snapshots in non-equilibrium dynamics
summary: We study non-equilibrium quantum dynamics by performing principal component analysis on the data sets of wavefunction snapshots. We show that a specific transformation of the data sets maximizes the information content in the largest principal component and further enables its connection to certain observables. This connection enables us to explain the dynamical features revealed by such a dimensionality-reduction scheme. We demonstrate this using quantum dynamics of the Heisenberg spin chain, starting from different initial states, and further extend the approach to extract higher-order correlations. Our framework should also be applicable to other unsupervised machine-learning methods based on dimensionality-reduction schemes and is highly relevant to experiments with quantum simulators, including those in higher dimensions.

AI.tldr: PCA on wavefunction snapshots reveals dynamical features in quantum systems via dimensionality reduction, connecting largest principal components to observables.
AI.motivation: To develop a framework for analyzing non-equilibrium quantum dynamics using unsupervised machine learning, specifically dimensionality reduction techniques like PCA, that can extract meaningful physical information from wavefunction snapshots and connect to observables.
AI.method: Principal component analysis (PCA) applied to datasets of wavefunction snapshots from quantum dynamics simulations, with a specific transformation to maximize information content in the largest principal component. Demonstrated on Heisenberg spin chain dynamics from different initial states, extended to extract higher-order correlations.
AI.result: The method successfully connects the largest principal component to certain observables, enabling explanation of dynamical features revealed by dimensionality reduction. The framework works for different initial states and can extract higher-order correlations.
AI.conclusion: The PCA-based framework provides an effective approach for analyzing quantum dynamics through dimensionality reduction, with potential applications to other unsupervised machine learning methods and relevance to quantum simulator experiments, including higher-dimensional systems.

title: Quantum memory on a nanophotonic silicon chip
summary: Integrated photonic circuits offer great promise for quantum technologies. However, due to the rapid propagation of light, many envisioned applications require efficient on-chip quantum memories with a programmable delay, compact footprint, and high fidelity. Implementing this based on standard semiconductor processing technology is an outstanding challenge. Here, we realize such memories using erbium-doped silicon waveguides, fabricated as part of a multi-wafer project by a nanophotonic foundry. We demonstrate light storage with a $44.2(9)\ \text{MHz}$ bandwidth and a programmable delay exceeding $1\ μ\text{s}$ in a device with a footprint of only $1.5\times 10^{-2}\ \text{mm}^2$, outperforming on-chip delay lines by many orders of magnitude. The phase of the read-out light field is preserved with a visibility of $91.3(30)\ \%$. The efficiency of $1.89(28)\times 10^{-8}$ can be improved in future devices through resonator enhancement and higher dopant concentrations. With this, the demonstrated approach will pave the way towards applications in photonic quantum computing based on scalable silicon processing technology.

AI.tldr: On-chip quantum memory using erbium-doped silicon waveguides achieves programmable delay >1 μs with 44.2 MHz bandwidth and 91.3% phase visibility in compact 0.015 mm² footprint.
AI.motivation: Integrated photonic circuits need efficient on-chip quantum memories with programmable delay, compact footprint, and high fidelity for quantum technologies, but implementing this with standard semiconductor processing is challenging.
AI.method: Use erbium-doped silicon waveguides fabricated by a nanophotonic foundry as part of a multi-wafer project to create on-chip quantum memories.
AI.result: Demonstrated light storage with 44.2 MHz bandwidth, programmable delay exceeding 1 μs in 0.015 mm² footprint, 91.3% phase visibility, and 1.89×10⁻⁸ efficiency, outperforming on-chip delay lines by orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: The approach paves the way for photonic quantum computing applications using scalable silicon processing technology, with potential improvements through resonator enhancement and higher dopant concentrations.

title: Detection of quantum imaginarity using moments and its interferometric realization
summary: Complex numbers, intrinsic to the formulation of quantum theory, play a pivotal role in enabling advantages across a broad range of quantum information-processing tasks. Despite their fundamental importance, practical and scalable criteria for detecting quantum imaginarity remain relatively underexplored, particularly methods that enable its identification with reduced experimental overhead. In this work, we propose a realistic and experimentally feasible method to detect quantum imaginarity using moment-based approach. Our framework relies on experimentally accessible moments of the Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability distribution, enabling scalable detection in many-body and high-dimensional systems without requiring full state tomography. We then present an illustrative example to support our detection scheme. Finally, we present an interferometric scheme for measuring these moments, paving the way for experimental implementation of our detection protocol.

AI.tldr: Proposes experimentally feasible moment-based detection of quantum imaginarity using Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability moments, enabling scalable detection without full tomography.
AI.motivation: Complex numbers are fundamental to quantum theory and enable quantum advantages, but practical, scalable methods for detecting quantum imaginarity remain underexplored, especially those with reduced experimental overhead.
AI.method: Uses moment-based approach with experimentally accessible moments of the Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability distribution, enabling scalable detection in many-body and high-dimensional systems without full state tomography. Includes illustrative example and interferometric measurement scheme.
AI.result: Developed a realistic and experimentally feasible framework for detecting quantum imaginarity using moments of Kirkwood-Dirac distribution, with proposed interferometric measurement scheme for experimental implementation.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, scalable method for detecting quantum imaginarity that reduces experimental overhead compared to full tomography, with proposed experimental implementation scheme.

title: Multi-Mode Quantum Annealing for Variational Autoencoders with General Boltzmann Priors
summary: Variational autoencoders (VAEs) learn compact latent representations of complex data, but their generative capacity is fundamentally constrained by the choice of prior distribution over the latent space. Energy-based priors offer a principled way to move beyond factorized assumptions and capture structured interactions among latent variables, yet training such priors at scale requires accurate and efficient sampling from intractable distributions. Here we present Boltzmann-machine--prior VAEs (BM-VAEs) trained using quantum annealing--based sampling in three distinct operational modes within a single generative system. During training, diabatic quantum annealing (DQA) provides unbiased Boltzmann samples for gradient estimation of the energy-based prior; for unconditional generation, slower quantum annealing (QA) concentrates samples near low-energy minima; for conditional generation, bias fields are added to direct sampling toward attribute-specific regions of the energy landscape (c-QA). Using up to 2000 qubits on a D-Wave Advantage2 processor, we demonstrate stable and efficient training across multiple datasets, with faster convergence and lower reconstruction loss than a Gaussian-prior VAE. The learned Boltzmann prior enables unconditional generation by sampling directly from the energy-based latent distribution, a capability that plain autoencoders lack, and conditional generation through latent biasing that leverages the learned pairwise interactions.

AI.tldr: BM-VAEs combine variational autoencoders with energy-based Boltzmann priors trained using quantum annealing sampling across three modes: diabatic for training, slower for unconditional generation, and biased for conditional generation.
AI.motivation: Standard VAEs are limited by factorized prior distributions that cannot capture structured latent interactions. Energy-based priors offer more expressive modeling but require efficient sampling from intractable distributions, which quantum annealing can provide.
AI.method: Three-mode quantum annealing system: 1) Diabatic quantum annealing (DQA) for unbiased Boltzmann sampling during training gradient estimation, 2) Slower quantum annealing (QA) for unconditional generation near low-energy minima, 3) Conditional QA (c-QA) with bias fields for attribute-specific generation.
AI.result: Using up to 2000 qubits on D-Wave Advantage2, BM-VAEs achieve stable training across datasets with faster convergence and lower reconstruction loss than Gaussian-prior VAEs. The system enables both unconditional generation from the learned energy-based distribution and conditional generation through latent biasing.
AI.conclusion: Quantum annealing enables practical training of energy-based priors in VAEs, overcoming sampling challenges and providing a unified framework for training, unconditional generation, and conditional generation while capturing structured latent interactions.

title: Query Learning Nearly Pauli Sparse Unitaries in Diamond Distance
summary:
We study the problem of learning nearly $(s,ε)$-sparse unitaries, meaning that the Pauli spectrum is concentrated on at most $s$ components with at most $ε$ residual mass in Pauli $\ell_1$-norm. This class generalizes well-studied families, including sparse unitaries, quantum $k$-juntas, $2^k$-Pauli dimensional channels, and compositions of depth $O(\log\log n)$ circuits with near-Clifford circuits.
  Given query access to an unknown nearly sparse unitary $U$, our goal is to efficiently (both in time and query complexity) construct a quantum channel that is close in diamond distance to $U$. We design a learning algorithm achieving this guarantee using $\tilde{O}(s^6/ε^4)$ forward queries to $U$, and running time polynomial in relevant parameters.
  A key contribution is an efficient quantum algorithm that, given query access to an arbitrary unknown unitary $U$, estimates all Pauli coefficients (up to a shared global phase) whose magnitude exceeds a given threshold $θ$, extending existing sparse recovery techniques to general unitaries.
  We also study the broader class of unitaries with bounded Pauli $\ell_1$-norm. For that class, we prove an exponential query lower bound $Ω(2^{n/2})$. We introduce a more relaxed accuracy metric which is the diamond distance restricted to a set of input states. Then, we show that, under this metric, unitaries with Pauli $\ell_1$-norm uniformly bounded by $L_1$ are learnable with $\tilde{O}(L_1^8/ε^{16})$.

AI.tldr: The paper presents algorithms for learning nearly sparse unitaries with efficient query complexity, introduces a quantum algorithm for estimating significant Pauli coefficients, and establishes query lower bounds for unitaries with bounded Pauli ℓ₁-norm.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop efficient learning algorithms for quantum unitaries that are nearly sparse in their Pauli spectrum, which generalizes several important classes of quantum operations including sparse unitaries, quantum k-juntas, and certain circuit compositions. This addresses the challenge of characterizing unknown quantum operations with practical query and time complexity.
AI.method: The method involves: 1) Designing a learning algorithm for nearly (s,ε)-sparse unitaries using Õ(s⁶/ε⁴) forward queries, 2) Developing an efficient quantum algorithm that estimates all Pauli coefficients exceeding a threshold θ for arbitrary unitaries, extending sparse recovery techniques, 3) Studying unitaries with bounded Pauli ℓ₁-norm and proving exponential query lower bounds, 4) Introducing a relaxed accuracy metric (diamond distance restricted to input states) and showing learnability with Õ(L₁⁸/ε¹⁶) queries for this class.
AI.result: Key results include: 1) An efficient learning algorithm for nearly sparse unitaries with Õ(s⁶/ε⁴) query complexity, 2) A quantum algorithm for estimating significant Pauli coefficients in general unitaries, 3) An exponential query lower bound Ω(2^{n/2}) for unitaries with bounded Pauli ℓ₁-norm under standard diamond distance, 4) Learnability with Õ(L₁⁸/ε¹⁶) queries under the restricted diamond distance metric.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes fundamental results in quantum learning theory, showing that nearly sparse unitaries are efficiently learnable while general unitaries with bounded Pauli ℓ₁-norm require exponential queries under standard metrics. The introduction of a relaxed accuracy metric enables learning for a broader class with polynomial query complexity, demonstrating a trade-off between accuracy requirements and learnability.

title: Learning and Generating Mixed States Prepared by Shallow Channel Circuits
summary: Learning quantum states from measurement data is a central problem in quantum information and computational complexity. In this work, we study the problem of learning to generate mixed states on a finite-dimensional lattice. Motivated by recent developments in mixed state phases of matter, we focus on arbitrary states in the trivial phase. A state belongs to the trivial phase if there exists a shallow preparation channel circuit under which local reversibility is preserved throughout the preparation. We prove that any mixed state in this class can be efficiently learned from measurement access alone. Specifically, given copies of an unknown trivial phase mixed state, our algorithm outputs a shallow local channel circuit that approximately generates this state in trace distance. The sample complexity and runtime are polynomial (or quasi-polynomial) in the number of qubits, assuming constant (or polylogarithmic) circuit depth and gate locality. Importantly, the learner is not given the original preparation circuit and relies only on its existence. Our results provide a structural foundation for quantum generative models based on shallow channel circuits. In the classical limit, our framework also inspires an efficient algorithm for classical diffusion models using only a polynomial overhead of training and generation.

AI.tldr: Efficient learning algorithm for mixed quantum states in trivial phase using shallow channel circuits, with polynomial/quasi-polynomial sample complexity and runtime.
AI.motivation: Learning quantum states from measurement data is fundamental in quantum information. Recent developments in mixed state phases of matter motivate studying arbitrary states in trivial phases, which can be prepared by shallow circuits preserving local reversibility.
AI.method: Develop algorithm that learns mixed states in trivial phase from measurement access alone. Given copies of unknown trivial phase mixed state, outputs shallow local channel circuit that approximately generates the state in trace distance. Learner doesn't know original preparation circuit, only relies on its existence.
AI.result: Proves any mixed state in trivial phase class can be efficiently learned. Sample complexity and runtime are polynomial/quasi-polynomial in number of qubits, assuming constant/polylogarithmic circuit depth and gate locality.
AI.conclusion: Provides structural foundation for quantum generative models based on shallow channel circuits. In classical limit, inspires efficient algorithm for classical diffusion models with polynomial overhead for training and generation.

title: Building Block For Universal Continuous Variables Computation In Superconducting Devices
summary: Continuous variable (CV) quantum computation offers an alternative to qubit-based computing by exploiting the infinite-dimensional Hilbert space of bosonic modes. Despite recent progress, superconducting platforms have yet to demonstrate a scalable architecture capable of universal computation.Here, we design and numerically simulate a two-layer superconducting architecture that implements all five interactions of the universal CV gate set (rotation, displacement, squeezing, Kerr, and beam splitter) within experimentally accessible regimes. To this end, we employ a DC-SQUID as the bosonic mode, a fluxonium qubit to mediate nonlinear interactions, and two ancillary qubits that enable Gaussian and multi-mode operations. By tuning fluxes and frequencies, we achieve high fidelities ($\geq 98\%$) across all gates within state-of-the-art parameter ranges. The modular nature of the design allows straightforward scaling, establishing a feasible pathway toward high-fidelity, universal CV quantum computation based on superconducting circuits.

AI.tldr: A superconducting architecture for universal continuous variable quantum computation using DC-SQUID bosonic modes, fluxonium qubits, and ancillary qubits achieves high-fidelity gates and scalable design.
AI.motivation: Continuous variable quantum computation offers advantages over qubit-based approaches but lacks scalable superconducting implementations. The paper addresses the need for a practical architecture that can perform universal CV operations with high fidelity.
AI.method: Designs a two-layer superconducting architecture using: 1) DC-SQUID as bosonic mode, 2) fluxonium qubit for nonlinear interactions, 3) two ancillary qubits for Gaussian and multi-mode operations. Achieves universal CV gate set (rotation, displacement, squeezing, Kerr, beam splitter) by tuning fluxes and frequencies within experimentally accessible regimes.
AI.result: Numerical simulations demonstrate high fidelities (≥98%) for all five universal CV gates using state-of-the-art parameter ranges. The modular design enables straightforward scaling.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture establishes a feasible pathway toward high-fidelity, universal continuous variable quantum computation based on superconducting circuits, addressing scalability challenges in CV quantum computing platforms.

title: A Security-Aware Nonlinearity Study of FPGA-Based Time-to-Digital Converters for Quantum Key Distribution Systems
summary: Intrinsic nonlinearity in FPGA-based time-to-digital converters (TDCs) is often treated as a calibration issue and evaluated mainly through post-correction metrics. In quantum key distribution (QKD), however, raw delay-line nonuniformity can affect coincidence timing and thereby influence accidental-coincidence rate and Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER). This paper analyzes how measured FPGA-TDC nonlinearity propagates to QKD timing metrics using a conservative system-level model that combines random timing uncertainty and deterministic nonlinearity. We also propose fabric-level mitigation strategies based on LUT-assisted delay shaping and placement constraints to reduce severe bin-width irregularities without statistical calibrations. The method is evaluated by reproducing two open-source TDCs implemented on a low-cost Zynq-7000 FPGA. We observe reductions of 14\%-21\% in integral nonlinearity (INL) compared with the non-optimized design, leading to a reduced QBER contribution and an improvement by 3.7\%-14.2\% in the estimated secret fraction. These results suggest that raw FPGA-TDC nonlinearity deserves explicit consideration in timing-sensitive QKD implementations.

AI.tldr: FPGA-based TDC nonlinearity affects QKD performance; proposed LUT-assisted delay shaping reduces INL by 14-21% and improves secret fraction by 3.7-14.2%.
AI.motivation: FPGA-TDC nonlinearity is typically treated as a calibration issue, but in QKD systems, raw delay-line nonuniformity directly impacts coincidence timing, affecting accidental-coincidence rates and QBER, requiring explicit consideration in timing-sensitive implementations.
AI.method: Combined conservative system-level model of random timing uncertainty and deterministic nonlinearity; proposed fabric-level mitigation using LUT-assisted delay shaping and placement constraints; evaluated by reproducing two open-source TDCs on Zynq-7000 FPGA.
AI.result: Reduced integral nonlinearity (INL) by 14-21% compared to non-optimized design, leading to reduced QBER contribution and 3.7-14.2% improvement in estimated secret fraction.
AI.conclusion: Raw FPGA-TDC nonlinearity deserves explicit consideration in timing-sensitive QKD implementations, and fabric-level mitigation strategies can significantly improve system performance without statistical calibrations.

title: 100 years of spin: fundamental physics, dark matter, exotic interactions, and all that
summary: For a century, spin has been an indispensable probe of the fundamental laws of nature. A reflection on the role of spin in shaping modern physics is presented, from the early days of quantum mechanics to the latest precision tests of the Standard Model. The significance of magnetic and electric dipole moments in testing CP and CPT symmetries is surveyed, along with the ongoing searches for exotic spin-dependent interactions that may reveal the nature of dark matter and its connection to spacetime geometry. Through these vignettes, it is shown that spin continues to provide a fresh perspective on the most profound questions in physics today.

AI.tldr: A century-long review of spin's role in fundamental physics, from quantum mechanics origins to modern precision tests of the Standard Model, CP/CPT symmetries, and dark matter searches.
AI.motivation: To reflect on the historical and ongoing significance of spin as a fundamental probe of nature's laws, demonstrating its continued relevance in addressing profound physics questions.
AI.method: Historical review and survey approach examining spin's role across different eras of physics, focusing on magnetic/electric dipole moments as symmetry testers and spin-dependent interactions as dark matter probes.
